section: ABSTRACT
text: A preliminary study was made of the dynamics and control of a 1000 Mw(e), single-fluid MSBR by an analog computer simulation.

section: 1. INTRODUCTION
text: By means of an analog computer simulation, a preliminary investigation was made of the dynamics and possibilities for control of the proposed 1000-Mw(e) single-fluid Molten-Salt Breeder Reactor (MSBR).

section: 2.1 Investigation of Plant Conditions for Less than Full-Load Operation
text: The primary objective of this study was to formulate a control system that would maintain the temperature of the steam delivered to the turbines at a design value of $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ during all steady-state conditions and to within a narrow band around this value during plant transients.

section: 2.1 Investigation of Plant Conditions for Less than Full-Load Operation
text: 5.1.) Three basic methods of plant operation at less than full load were investigated:

section: 2.1 Investigation of Plant Conditions for Less than Full-Load Operation
text: The average reactor temperature was held at its $100\%$ power level value, and a secondary-salt bypass line was included in parallel with the steam generators.

section: 2.1 Investigation of Plant Conditions for Less than Full-Load Operation
text: With the first two methods of plant operation the temperature of the secondary salt approached its freezing point of $725^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ at power levels $\geq 50\%$ of full power.

section: 2.1 Investigation of Plant Conditions for Less than Full-Load Operation
text: With the second method, the $\Delta T$ from primary to secondary salt in the primary heat exchanger increased from 150 to $335^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ at the primary-salt exit end of the exchanger at $50\%$ power, and the $\Delta T$ increased at the steam outlet end of the steam generator as well.

section: 2.1 Investigation of Plant Conditions for Less than Full-Load Operation

section: Ornl Dwg. 69-6634
text: A brief parameter study of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ indicated that a reasonable steam-temperature response was obtained when a $1^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ steam-temperature error produced a secondary-salt flow rate change of $10\%/\min$ and a $1\%$ error in flow rate produced a primary-salt temperature change of $1^{\circ}\mathrm{F}/\min$ .

section: Ornl Dwg. 69-6634
text: The maximum change in the secondary-salt flow rate was about $20\%$ , i.e., from 100 to $80\%$ of the design value; the maximum rate of change was about $50\%/\min$ .

section: Ornl Dwg. 69-6634
text: The flow rate returned to within $3\%$ of its design value in approximately 250 sec (4.2 min).

section: Ornl Dwg. 69-6634

section: 3. RESULTS
text: Several transient cases were run which included (1) decreases in load demand $P_{\mathrm{r}}$ from 100% by steps of 10, 30, 50, and 60%; (2) ramp changes of 30 and 70% each at 5 and 10%/min; (3) changes in reactivity of steps of ±0.05, ±0.1, and -0.5%; and (4), with the reactivity controller disconnected, (a) reactivity steps of ±0.05, +0.01, and -0.1% and (b) ramp changes in reactivity of +0.05 and -0.1% at 0.1%/min.

section: 3.1 Decrease in Load Demand
text: A new steady-state operation was achieved when the steam temperature reached its design value of $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and the secondary-salt flow rate its full-power value.

section: 3.1 Decrease in Load Demand
text: The results of a $30\%$ decrease in load demand from $100\%$ as a step and at rates of 10 and $5\% / \min$ are shown in Figs.

section: 3.1 Decrease in Load Demand
text: For large, rapid changes in load demand, the temperature of the secondary-salt may approach its freezing point $(725^{\circ}\mathrm{F})$ .

section: 3.1 Decrease in Load Demand
text: Since this flow may increase only to $110\%$ of full flow, a sufficiently rapid load increase will cause the temperature of the secondary salt leaving the steam generator to decrease and, perhaps, approach the freezing point.

text: For Step Losses of Load Demand (from 100%)

text: A plot was made of the maximum steam temperature variation from $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ as a function of change in load demand (Fig.

section: 3.1 Decrease in Load Demand
text: The plot shows, for example, that a step change of $30\%$ in load demand from 100 to $70\%$ of full power produced a maximum steam temperature deviation of about $+42^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ at some point in the transient.

section: 3.1 Decrease in Load Demand
text: The break upwards in the three curves for load changes greater than $30\%$ was caused by the $40\%$ lower limit imposed on the secondary-salt flow rate.

section: 3.1 Decrease in Load Demand
text: Changes in load of more than $30\%$ required a change of greater than $60\%$ in the secondary-salt flow rate to maintain control of the steam temperature.

section: 3.2 Changes of Reactivity
text: The results of reactivity steps of $\pm 0.1$ and $-0.5\%$ are shown in Figs.

section: 3.2 Changes of Reactivity
text: A $+0.1\%$ step yielded a peak reactor power of about $155\%$ as a pulse with a fwhm (full-width, half maximum) of about 0.75 sec.

section: 3.2 Changes of Reactivity
text: This is an excess energy input of approximately 930 Mw-sec.

section: 3.2 Changes of Reactivity
text: The reactor outlet temperature peak deviation from $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ was about $25^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

section: 3.2 Changes of Reactivity
text: A negative reactivity insertion of $-0.5\%$ decreased the reactor power sharply to about $18\%$ before the control reactivity returned it to its $100\%$ level after a $35\%$ overshoot (Fig.

section: 3.2 Changes of Reactivity
text: The reactor outlet temperature peak change was about $-100^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , and the peak steam temperature variation was about $-15^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

section: 3.3 Step Changes of Reactivity with Controller Disconnected
text: With the reactivity controller disconnected so that a reactor power-level error produced no response from the reactivity servo (i.e., no control-rod motion), step changes of $+0.05$ and $-0.1\%$ were studied (Figs.

section: 3.3 Step Changes of Reactivity with Controller Disconnected
text: The $+0.05\%$ step change produced a peak reactor power of about $147\%$ , and the reactor temperatures increased to a value commensurate with the negative temperature coefficient of reactivity.

section: 3.3 Step Changes of Reactivity with Controller Disconnected
text: The secondary-salt flow rate decreased to its $40\%$ lower limit, after which steam temperature control was lost.

text: The final steam temperature deviation was $+75^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

text: This temperature decreased to as low as $690^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ during the transient, returning to a steady-state value of approximately $735^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

section: 3.5 Step Loss of One Secondary-Salt Coolant Loop
text: We call the reactor core inlet temperature $\hat{T}_{1}$ , and the primary-salt outlet temperatures from the four primary heat exchangers $T_{11}, T_{12}, T_{13}$ and $T_{14}$ ; then, with perfect mixing

section: 3.5 Step Loss of One Secondary-Salt Coolant Loop
text: We now assume that if one of the primary heat exchangers, e.g., number 4, suddenly were to cease to remove heat from the primary salt, the reduction in heat removal would appear as a step increase in the primary-salt outlet temperature $T_{14}$ from 1050 to $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

section: 3.5 Step Loss of One Secondary-Salt Coolant Loop
text: Thus, the reactor core inlet temperature would be changed as a step at $t = 0$ from $T_{1}$ to $T_{1}$ .

section: 3.5 Step Loss of One Secondary-Salt Coolant Loop
text: If one secondary-salt loop were to be lost suddenly, four steam generators would be supplied heat no longer, say generators 13, 14, 15, and 16.

section: 3.5 Step Loss of One Secondary-Salt Coolant Loop
text: We assume that this reduction would appear as a step decrease in the steam generator outlet temperatures $T_{7,13}$ , $T_{7,14}$ , $T_{7,15}$ and $T_{7,16}$ from 1000 to $700^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

section: 3.5 Step Loss of One Secondary-Salt Coolant Loop
text: Thus, the turbine steam temperature would be changed as a step at $t = 0$ from $T_7$ to $T_7$ .

section: 3.5 Step Loss of One Secondary-Salt Coolant Loop
text: The turbine steam temperature dropped as a step to $925^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , and the reactor inlet temperature rose to $1113^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

section: 3.5 Step Loss of One Secondary-Salt Coolant Loop
text: The control rods were withdrawn (inserting negative reactivity) and, with the aid of the negative primary-salt reactivity temperature coefficient, the reactor power dropped sharply to $70\%$ .

section: 3.5 Step Loss of One Secondary-Salt Coolant Loop
text: The steam flow rate increased to its $110\%$ limit in an attempt to meet the $100\%$ load demand at the reduced steam temperature.

section: 3.5 Step Loss of One Secondary-Salt Coolant Loop
text: The secondary-salt flow rate increased to its $110\%$ limit in an attempt to maintain the steam temperature at $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

section: 3.5 Step Loss of One Secondary-Salt Coolant Loop
text: With the loss of $25\%$ of the heat transfer capability in the secondary loop, the reactor temperatures began to rise to meet the $100\%$ load demand.

section: 3.5 Step Loss of One Secondary-Salt Coolant Loop
text: However, when the reactor outlet temperature reached its $1400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ limit, the reactor power had increased to only $92\%$ of full load, and the steam temperature had increased to $956^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

section: 3.5 Step Loss of One Secondary-Salt Coolant Loop
text: Therefore, under this accident condition and with the temperature and flow limits placed on the system, the plant could not deliver $100\%$ of full load with one secondary-salt loop inoperable.

section: 3.5 Step Loss of One Secondary-Salt Coolant Loop
text: Figure 15 also shows the result of decreasing the load demand to $75\%$ at a rate of $10\%/\mathrm{min}$ beginning 325 sec after the step loss of heat transfer capacity.

section: 3.6 Measurement of System Transfer Function
text: The curve for the case with no controller is similar to previous data for the two-fluid MSBR,2 although the magnitude of the peak gain at about 3 radians/sec ( $\sim 0.5 \, \text{Hz}$ ) was greater by a factor of about 6 for the single-fluid case.

section: 3.6 Measurement of System Transfer Function
text: Adding the servo controller greatly decreased the low-frequency gain, as expected; above the upper cutoff frequency of 5 Hz ( $\sim 30 \, \text{radians/sec}$ ) for the servo it had no effect.

section: 4. CONCLUDING REMARKS
text: We have described a preliminary investigation of the control problems associated with the 1000-Mw(e) single-fluid MSBR.
